coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-shielded Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F@C nanocuboids as robust cathode for Na-ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Gang
Chao, Dongliang
Guo, Yuwei
Chen, Zhen
Wang, Huanhuan
Savilov, Serguei V.
Lin, Jianyi
Shen, Ze Xiang
coredata.dc:description: The natural abundance, environmental friendliness, low cost of sodium salts, and the electrochemical properties similarity between lithium- and sodium-ion batteries (LIBs, SIBs) make SIBs one of the most promising alternatives to current LIBs. In order to match with performances of LIBs, challenges in improving high-rate capability and long-term cycling stability of SIBs need to be addressed. In this work, we design and construct an excellent cathode material for SIB, i.e. carbon-coated, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) shielded Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F nanocuboids (NVOPF@C), which possesses highly exposed crystalline a–b planes for rapid ion diffusion. The GQD as well as amorphous carbon layer shielding NVOPF nanocuboids not only can improve the charge transport and transfer kinetics, but also stabilize the open framework structure from the agglomeration. Impressively, NVOPF@C delivers an unexpectedly high capacity of 70mAhg−1 at ultra-high rate 45C, with 81% retention after 2000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium ion battery
Cathode
Sodium vanadium fluorophosphate
Graphene quantum dots
Express carbon coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the lattice structure of NVOPF crystals. (b,c) Schematic illustration of NVOPF and NVOPF@C, and their related SEM images.

TEM characterizations. (a,b) TEM and HRTEM of NVOPF@C nanocuboid. (c) HRTEM with selected FFT pattern of GQDs in inset. (d) Dark-field TEM image of NVOPF@C nanocuboid and its corresponding EDS mapping of Na, V, O, P, F and C elements.
(a,b) TGA curve and EDX spectrum of NVOPF@C sample. (c,d) XRD patterns and Raman spectra of NVOPF and NVOPF@C samples.

Electrochemical properties of both NVOPF and NVOPF@C. (a) Initial charge–discharge voltage profiles at 0.2C. (b) CV curves at 1mvs−1. (c,d) The charge–discharge curves of NVOPF and NVOPF@C at different rates. The yellow points represent the estimated average discharge plateaus E
mid . (e) Rate capability at various rates from 0.1C to 45C. (f) Nyquist plot at full-charged state after 5 cycles at 0.1C. The resistance is simulated using the equivalent circuit R

(a) Low rate cycling performance of both NVOPF and NVOPF@C electrodes. (b) High rate long-term cycling for GQDs-anchored NVOPF@C electrode. (c) Performance comparison with other reported sodium-vanadium fluorophosphates family. The captions at the triangle vertices represent the highest values including our finding (the red triangle). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-shielded Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F@C nanocuboids as robust cathode for Na-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Engineering, Panzhihua University, 617000 Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Engineering, Panzhihua University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University, 637371, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Moscow State University, 119992 Moscow, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Moscow State University
School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The natural abundance, environmental friendliness, low cost of sodium salts, and the electrochemical properties similarity between lithium- and sodium-ion batteries (LIBs, SIBs) make SIBs one of the most promising alternatives to current LIBs. In order to match with performances of LIBs, challenges in improving high-rate capability and long-term cycling stability of SIBs need to be addressed. In this work, we design and construct an excellent cathode material for SIB, i.e. carbon-coated, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) shielded Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F nanocuboids (NVOPF@C), which possesses highly exposed crystalline a–b planes for rapid ion diffusion. The GQD as well as amorphous carbon layer shielding NVOPF nanocuboids not only can improve the charge transport and transfer kinetics, but also stabilize the open framework structure from the agglomeration. Impressively, NVOPF@C delivers an unexpectedly high capacity of 70mAhg−1 at ultra-high rate 45C, with 81% retention after 2000 cycles.

Graphene quantum dots anchored [001]-oriented Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F@C nanocuboid was successful synthesized which is a high-performance positive electrode for Na-ion storage. The findings highlight the synergetic effect of oriented-crystallinity, morphology control and surface modification to fast kinetics and long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion battery (LIB) is currently the most popular type of rechargeable batteries for portable electronics and electrical vehicles [1–6]. The increasing demand of Li has led to a dramatic consumption of lithium sources and hence the cost rises. Considerable amount of efforts has been dedicated to searching alternative elements for high performance batteries, in particular for large-scale stationary energy storage. Sodium is a promising candidate, not only due to its high availability and low cost, but also because of the close resemblance of its ion intercalation characters to those of lithium [7–9]. Although the less reduction potential (−2.71 vs. −3.05V) and larger ion size (0.095 vs. 0.068nm) of Na+ vs. Li+ may lead to slightly lower energy density of sodium ion battery (SIB), the rate capacity of SIB is actually compatible to LIB, such as Na3V2(PO4)3/graphene, [10] Sb@TiO2−x, [11] mesoporous MoS2, [12] and SnS nano-honeycomb [13]. In particular, at high C-rates larger size of Na+ is in favor of Na ion insertion kinetics due to lower viscosity, desolvation energy, and diffusion activation energy of the Na+-based electrolyte [14]. Up to now, various types of cathode materials have been extensively explored for SIBs, which include layered transition metal oxides, phosphates, fluophosphates, and some other sodium insertion materials such as FeF3 [14–18]. Among them sodium-vanadium fluorophosphates, Na3V2O2x(PO4)2F3−2x (0≤x≤1) are attractive due to their excellent properties, including the highest energy density reported, the high operation voltages (3.6–4V) and excellent cycling life due to small volume change (1.8%) during sodium ion intercalation [19–21]. Compared to Na3V2(PO4)2F3 (x=0, NVPF), the charge/discharge plateau voltages of Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F (x=1, NVOPF) are theoretically a bit lower due to weaker inductive effects from fewer F− anions [19,22]. However, the less F− anions contains, the weaker electronegativity and attraction interaction between Na2–F2. Hence experimentally NVOPF performs better due to a lower electrochemical polarization and exhibits faster Na+ ions diffusion as compared to NVPF [21]. Therefore, main research focuses have been switched to NVOPF [23].

NVOPF was first reported in 2002 by Massa et al. via hydrothermal synthesis [24]. In 2012 Rojo's group presented a capacity of 102mAhg−1 at C/20 and 68mAhg−1 at 1C. The high-rate capability and long-term cycling life were unsatisfactory in comparison with those of high-performance LIB cathodes because of its poor electronic conductivity ca. 1.8×10−7 Scm–1 [25]. Recently, Goodenough et al. employed nanoscale NVOPF/RGO structure to increase the conductivity and contact surface area of cathode with electrolyte so as to reduce sodium ion diffusion length by tailored nanomaterial design and surface engineering. As a result, discharge capacities of ca. 120mAhg−1 at C/20 and 60mAhg−1 at 5C were achieved [25]. Other surface engineering treatments, such as carbon wrapping [26] and RuO2 coating [20] were also reported to further enhance the rate performance. Nevertheless, challenges are still confronted for a facile, scalable and economical way to improve the rate and cycling performances.

In this work, three strategies were adopted to enhance the rate and cycling performance of NVOPF: i) Controlled growth of NVOPF in preferred [001] orientation enables shortening of ionic transportation in a–b plane to facilitate the rapid desodiation/sodiation during charge/discharge. As shown in Fig. 1a the NVOPF lattice consists of [VO5F] octahedra and [PO4] tetrahedra which are alternatively stacked in the a–b planes. They are connected with each other by oxygen vertices within the layers, while fluorine vertices of [VO5F] serve as bridges between the layers along the c axis [27]. It has been proved that Na+ moves in two-dimensional paths along the a–b plane during charge/discharge. In this work, by manipulating the pH value of the solution in solvothermal process, we could control the growth of NVOPF nanoparticles preferentially along the c-axis, i.e. the [001] direction, leading to the formation of NVOPF nanocuboids. The shorter diffusion length in the a–b plane should favor the intercalation/extraction of Na+ [20]. ii) Coating of graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 3-5nm in size) on the surface of NVOPF nanocuboids (see Fig. 1b) not only prevents the NVOPF nanocuboids from agglomeration, but also enhances the ionic and electric conductivity for rapid charge transfer [28,29]. iii) Uniform carbon coating on top of GQDs-shielded NVOPF (see Fig. 1c) further improves the structural stability and electronic conductivity of NVOPF nanoparticles [26,30]. As shown below, our NVOPF@C nanocuboids present a reversible capacity of 70mAhg−1 at 45C and more than 80% retention after 2000 cycles, which are the best rate capacity and cycle stability reported so far for the sodium-vanadium fluorophosphates SIB cathode, to the best of our knowledge. GQDs, as an emerging attractive carbon material, have been proved effective in supercapacitors, [31] Li-ion batteries, [28,32] solar cells, [33,34] and oxygen reduction reaction, [35] but seldom reported for its application in SIB. Carbon coating in this work was implemented by a facile and scalable annealing process. They are new methodologies desirable for tailored nanomaterial design and surface engineering of active materials.

The morphologies of both NVOPF and NVOPF@C compounds are revealed by SEM in Fig. 1b and c, respectively. NVOPF presents a cuboid-shaped nanostructure by the pH control of the solution [20]. The TEM images shown in Fig. 2a and b confirm the anisotropic growth along the c direction of the crystal, with edge lengths of 100-150nm along a- and b-axis, and ca. 500nm in c-direction. The short lengths of NVOPF nanocuiod in the a–b plane and preferred growth along [001] direction are favorable to the fast Na ions transport, resulting in enhanced reaction kinetics. In the HRTEM image GQDs (with a size of around 5nm) are unexpectedly observed on surface of NVOPF (see Fig. 2c). They were generated by solvent cutting and reducing of the small amount of GO which were added into the DMF solution during the solvothermal synthesis [28,36]. There are also observable few pieces of remaining reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (see Fig. 1b, c) because of the limitation of the cutting effect. The GQDs here serve as a dispersing agent to get evenly distributed NVOPF nanoparticles. The HRTEM results show good crystallinity with lattice distance of 2.5Å, corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [37,38]. The GQDs covering can effectively homogenize the distribution of NVOPF nanoparticles. On the contrary, the NVOPF particles without graphene shielding are keen to aggregate leading to big size particles and hence poor SIB kinetics and rate performance (see Figs. S1 a,b and S3). Moreover, the “bonding-unsaturation” feature of the GQDs layers can provide extra Na+ or electrolyte storage venues between the nanocavities of GQDs.

Each NVOPF nanocuboid was further coated with an amorphous carbon shell to further improve its electric conductivity and sodium-storage properties by a facile annealing process with 15min annealing of NVOPF at 400°C in a dilute ethylene glycol stream as described in experimental section. The morphologies and size of NVOPF@C nanocuboids remain consistent after the carbon coating (Figs. 1c and 2a, d). In Fig. 2b, the carbon layer is uniformly distributed on the surface of the nanocuboid with a controlled thickness around 3nm in consideration of short Na+ diffusion path (see Fig. S4 with thicker carbon coating). The total carbon weight in NVOPF@C nanocuboids is about 7.3wt% by TGA (see Fig. 3a). The onset burning temperature of GQDs is known to be lower than that of amorphous carbon (260 vs. 450°C), [39,40] which may enable the estimation of the weight percentage for GQDs (~2%) and amorphous carbon (~5.3%) coatings. The element analyses shown in Figs. 2d and 3b reveals an uniform distribution of all the elements in NVOPF@C with an elemental ratio about 3.02:2.00:2.03:0.98 for Na:V:P:F, which is consistent with the target composition.

The crystal structure of NVOPF and NVOPF@C was verified by powder XRD (Fig. 3c). All the diffraction patterns are consistent with the standard pattern for tetragonal Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F (PDF 76-3645), space group I4/mmm with lattice parameters of a=b=6.37Å and c=10.64Å [24,26]. No typical characteristic peaks of GQDs and carbon are found, suggesting that the GQDs may be below the detectable limit and the carbon coating introduced from ethylene glycol is amorphous. Raman spectra of NVOPF and NVOPF@C were conducted to further verify the existence of GQDs and NVOPF (see Fig. 3d). The characteristic signals at 1350 and 1600cm−1 correspond to the disorder-induced phonon mode (D-band) and graphite band (G-band), respectively. The intensity ratio of the D to G band (I
D
/I
G ) for NVOPF (I
D
/I
G =1.09) is larger than that of NVOPF@C (I
D
/I
G =0.89), indicating a higher degree of carbon network ordering and thus higher electronic conductivity of NVOPF@C with the help of ethylene glycol reduction [41,42]. The intense peaks at 938, 380, and 1043cm−1 shown in both NVOPF and NVOPF@C samples correspond to of A1g symmetric P–O stretching vibration band v 1, v 2 (E) mode, and antisymmetric stretching bands v 3 of the tetrahedral PO4 3− anion, respectively. The peaks between 530 and 660cm−1 attribute to v 4 vibrations [25]. The bands in low-frequency region (below 300cm−1) might be assigned to external modes of the [PO4] tetrahedra and/or [VO5F] octahedral [43]. By comparing the carbon signal (normalized with respect to A1g P–O stretching) in the two samples, the contents ratio of GQD and amorphous carbon can be estimated to be about 1:2.5, which is coincident with the previous TGA result.

Fig. 4a, b display the galvanostatic charge–discharge and CV curves of the NVOPF and NVOPF@C electrodes, respectively, over the potential range of 2–4.3V vs. Na/Na+. In Fig. 4a, the charging/discharging profiles of both samples are almost identical. Two high discharge plateau potentials locates at ca. 4.0V and 3.6V respectively, which suggests the same mechanism for the two-step desodiation/sodiation in the following equations:
(1) Na3(VO)2(PO4)2F↔Na2(VO)2(PO4)2F+Na++e−
(2) Na2(VO)2(PO4)2F↔Na(VO)2(PO4)2F+Na++e−

Little overpotential gaps are observed between two pairs of charge/discharge plateaus in Fig. 4a, demonstrating good reversibility and small polarization of the electrodes. At low discharge rate of 0.2C, only slightly higher discharge capacity for NVOPF@C (vs. NVOPF) can be found. However, with increasing charge/discharge rates from 0.2C up to 45C, the rate performance decay of NVOPF vs. NVOPF@C is getting more evident. In Fig. 4c–e, the low mass polarization tendency of NVOPFF@C electrode is clearly demonstrated. The specific capacity of NVOPF@C electrode at 45C (70mAhg−1) is three times higher than that of NVOPF electrode (23mAhg−1). A ~85% initial coulombic efficiency is obtained for NVOPF@C electrode, while nearly 100% retention is maintained after the first 5 cycles. The CV curves of NVOPF and NVOPF@C are coincided with each other at low scan rate of 0.1mVs−1 (see Fig. S5). With increasing of the scan rate to 1mVs−1, the peak intensity of NVOPF becomes lower and the peak shifts begins (Figs. 4b and S6). Through comparison, carbon coating enhances the electronic conductivity, leading to reduced polarization, high reversibility and improved rate capability.

The above argument is further supported by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. In the Nyquist plots (Fig. 4f), each impedance spectrum consists of a depressed semicircle at high-to-medium frequency region and a Warburg line at low frequency range. The EIS spectra are fitted with the equivalent circuit shown in inset Fig. 4f. R
S represents the internal resistance, including electrolyte and current collector/electrode interface contact resistances. R
int and R
ct , parallel to the constant phase element (CPE), refer to charge transport resistance (electronic and ionic conduction through the active electrode materials) and charge (electron)-transfer impedance, respectively. Warburg element (W
o ) is related to ion diffusion in the solid electrode. The simulated data from the equivalent circuit well fits the impedance data for both NVOPF and NVOPF@C. The electrolyte resistance is similar for NVOPF and NVOPF@C samples. However, the charge-transfer resistance of NVOPF (136Ω) is higher than that of NVOPF@C (87Ω). Moreover, the Na+ diffusion coefficient DNa+ of the NVOPF@C electrode is calculated to be ca. 3×10−10 cm2 s−1 by Randles–Sevchik equation, which is 3 times larger than that of the NVOPF (see Fig. S7) [44]. These results prove that the carbon coating can effectively improve charge transfer and ion transport kinetics.

The GQDs shielded NVOPF nanocuboids have greatly enhanced long cycle stability because of the improved charge conductivity and likely shielding effect of carbon coating in preventing dissolution of vanadium ions. As shown in Fig. 5a, our NVOPF@C electrode delivers retention of 92% of the original capacity after 500 cycles at 1C. Even the NVOPF electrode without carbon coating also presents capacity retention of 88%. To demonstrate the potential of high-power application of NVOPF@C electrode, we tested the long-term ultrafast charge/discharge ability (see Fig. 5b). After 2000 cycles at 45C, the discharge capacity can be stabilized ca. 57mAhg−1, delivering over 80% capacity retention. The coulombic efficiency is also close to 100%, suggesting good electrochemical reversibility and structure stability even at long-term high-rate cycling. Such excellent high-rate capability and cycling tolerance are superior to previous SIB cathodes reported to date (see comparison in Table S1 and Fig. 5c), which can be attributed to not only improved kinetics introduced by GODs and carbon coating, but also the robust structure of GQDs shielded NVOPF nanocuboid with large interstitial space perpendicularly to [001] direction for ultrafast Na+ uptakes.

In summary, we successfully synthesized GQDs-shielded NVOPF@C nanocuboids with preferred oriented growth along [001] direction as a cathode material for Na-ion batteries. The nanocuboid presents discharge capacity of 127mAhg−1 at 0.2C and is able to maintain a high capacity of 70mAhg−1 even at 45C. It also retains an ultra-long cycling life (never-reported) with high capacity retention of 81% after 2000 cycles. Our results demonstrate the power of this synthetic strategy in the anisotropic feature of NVOPF nanocuboid, facilitating faster insertion/desertion of sodium ions into the crystalline (002) facets along the a- and b-axes. GQDs doping not only prevents nanocuboids from aggregation during the synthesis process and long-term charge–discharge cycling, it also improves charge (both electronic and ionic) mobility for fast redox reactions. The electronic conductivity and structure stability are further enhanced by a simple and facile annealing carbon coating. These configurations propose a promising and effective strategy aiming at high power and long life sodium ion electrodes.

NVOPF@C nanocuboids were prepared in two steps. Firstly, graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-NVOPF composite material was synthesized by a solvothermal method. NH4H2PO4, NH4VO3, Na2CO3, and NaF were dissolved in 8mL graphene oxide solution (ca. 1mgmL−1) in a molar ratio of 1:1:0.5:0.5 to form a 0.75molL−1 stabilized solution. The mixed solution was then added to 20mL N,N-dimethylform amide (DMF) to form transparent microemulsion. The pH value was adjusted to 7 with HNO3. The resulting solution was transferred into a 30mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 20h. After repeatedly washing the as-prepared products with DI water and ethanol, we got the NVOPF black powder. This GQD-shielded NVOPF sample is denoted as NVOPF in the context. In the second step, an amorphous carbon layer was coated onto the obtained NVOPF powder ex-situ via a rapid annealing process. NVOPF powder was placed in a corundum crucible at the center of a quartz-tube furnace while 1mL ethylene glycol as the carbon source was loaded in an alumina boat at the upstream zone of the quartz tube. Before heating, the quartz tube was flushed with an Ar gas at 120sccm for 20min. The system was then ramped to 400°C within 20min, maintained at 400°C for 15min, and then cooled down to room temperature naturally under a 60sccm Ar flow. This carbon-coated sample is denoted as NVOPF@C.

The crystal structures of the samples were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RigakuD/Max-2550 with Cu Kα radiation). Raman spectra were recoded using a WITec-CRM200 Raman system (WITec, Germany) with an excitation laser source of wavelength of 532nm (2.33eV). The morphologies of the samples were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy. The architectures of the samples were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2010F at 200kV). TGA was conducted under a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 in air with a flowing rate of 25mL/min.

The working electrodes were prepared by mixing 85wt% prepared samples, 10wt% acetylene black and 5wt% polyvinylidene fluoride binder dissolved in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone. Standard CR2032-type coin cells were assembled in an Argon-filled glovebox (Mbraun, Germany). Metallic sodium foil was used as the counter-electrode, and 1M NaClO4 dissolved in a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC), propylene carbonate (PC) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) (1:1:0.05 in volume) was used as the electrolyte, and glass fiber was used as the separator. The cells were firstly galvanostatically charged/discharged in the voltage window of 4.3–2.0V versus Na/Na+ at 0.1C for activation (1C was defined as 130mAg−1 according to the theoretically capacity of NVOPF). The CV measurements were carried out through a Solartron electrochemical potentiostat at different scanning rates. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the amplitude of the sine perturbation signal was 5mV with scanning from the highest frequency (100kHz) to the lowest (10mHz). Galvanostatic charge–discharge cycles were tested by Neware battery tester at different current densities at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.ensm.2016.07.007.
